comments.text: While the full limits remain open, a rigorous analysis of just the 2-term consecutive sums establishes strict, unconditional boundaries that squeeze the possible behavior of the sequence. 1. An unconditional ceiling on the density and harmonic sum Let $A(x) = \#\{n : a_n < x\}$. By tracking excluded 2-term sums across sliding windows, we can unconditionally prove that $A(x) \le \frac{2}{3}x + O(\log x)$. This establishes two immediate corollaries: First, the upper density of any such sequence cannot exceed 2/3 (or 24/36). This leaves a beautifully narrow gap above Freud's 19/36 construction, showing exactly how close Freud got to the theoretical ceiling strictly from 2-term exclusions. Second, by standard partial summation ($\int_1^x t^{-1} dA(t)$), the harmonic sum is strictly bounded by $\sum_{a_n<x} \frac{1}{a_n} \le \frac{2}{3} \log x + O(1)$. 2. Proof of the 2/3 bound Let $y \ge 1$ be an integer. Consider the sequence elements inside the interval $[y, 2y-1]$. Let there be $k = A(2y) - A(y)$ such elements, denoted $b_1 < b_2 < \dots < b_k$. The $k-1$ sums of consecutive terms $b_i + b_{i+1}$ are strictly increasing and thus distinct. Because $b_1 \ge y$ and $b_k \le 2y-1$, all $k-1$ of these sums lie strictly in the target interval $[2y, 4y-2]$. By the rules of the sequence, none of these $k-1$ sums can belong to the sequence. The target interval $[2y, 4y-1]$ contains exactly $2y$ integers, meaning it can contain at most $2y - (k-1)$ sequence elements. Therefore: $A(4y) - A(2y) \le 2y - (A(2y) - A(y) - 1)$ Rearranging this yields a clean telescoping inequality: $A(4y) - A(y) \le 2y + 1$ If we apply this iteratively to $y = x/4, x/16, x/64 \dots$ and sum the inequalities, the left side telescopes to $A(x)$ and the right side becomes a geometric series: $A(x) \le 2 \left(\frac{x}{4} + \frac{x}{16} + \dots \right) + O(\log x) = \frac{2}{3}x + O(\log x)$ 3. A heuristic for why $\limsup a_n/n = \infty$ is likely true The 2/3 bound uses only 2-term sums. In reality, the sequence forbids $m$-term sums for all $m$. If the sequence maintained a consistent positive lower density $\epsilon > 0$, the set of $m$-term sums would have a local density of roughly $\epsilon/m$. Because the sequence forbids all of these sets, they must all perfectly co-locate in the $1-\epsilon$ density complement of the sequence. Since the sum of their densities $\sum \epsilon/m$ diverges logarithmically, these forbidden sets would have to overlap with near-perfect efficiency to allow the sequence to survive. This combinatorial rigidity strongly suggests that regions of high density create "waves" of forbidden integers that eventually starve the sequence, forcing the local density to plunge to 0 infinitely often. This implies $\liminf A(x)/x = 0$, which guarantees $\limsup a_n/n = \infty$. Note: The core mathematical analysis in this comment, specifically the sliding-window optimization yielding the 2/3 bound and the heuristic, was developed with the assistance of AI.
An upper density of $>1/2$ was achieved by Freud. See [867] for the reference. (The infinite case is discussed at the end of the journal article.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
